questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the paediatric clinic with multiple brown patches on his skin since infancy. Examination reveals 8 café-au-lait spots >5 mm, axillary freckling, and 2 Lisch nodules on slit-lamp examination. There is no family history of similar features. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of axillary freckling and Lisch nodules distinguishes NF1 from Legius syndrome (which lacks these features). McCune-Albright has irregular hyperpigmentation and endocrine anomalies, not Lisch nodules. Segmental neurofibromatosis is unilateral. Tuberous sclerosis features ash-leaf spots, not café-au-lait spots.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with progressive hearing loss and tinnitus in both ears. MRI reveals bilateral cerebellopontine angle masses consistent with vestibular schwannomas. She also has posterior subcapsular cataracts. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are diagnostic of NF2. Cataracts are a common associated feature. Schwannomatosis lacks vestibular involvement. Sporadic unilateral schwannomas do not present bilaterally. NF1 typically has neurofibromas, not schwannomas. Meningiomatosis describes multiple meningiomas, not schwannomas.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with multiple flesh-coloured, soft, painless lumps on the left forearm and hand only. He has no café-au-lait spots, no axillary freckling, and no Lisch nodules. There is no family history of similar lesions. Biopsy of one lump shows neurofibroma. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Localised neurofibromas in a single body region without systemic features or family history are typical of segmental neurofibromatosis. NF1 requires involvement of multiple systems. Schwannomatosis presents with painful schwannomas, not cutaneous neurofibromas. Lipomatosis and leiomyomatosis are not neurofibromas.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man is admitted with a rapidly enlarging, painful mass in his left thigh. MRI shows a fusiform tumour arising from the sciatic nerve. Biopsy confirms a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST). On closer examination, he has 7 café-au-lait spots >15 mm, axillary freckling, and two subcutaneous neurofibromas. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Sporadic malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour
questions.explanation: MPNST is a known complication of NF1. The presence of multiple café-au-lait spots, freckling, and neurofibromas confirms underlying NF1. Sporadic MPNST would lack these features. Li-Fraumeni presents with diverse tumours but not typical NF1 stigmata. Schwannomatosis and NF2 do not include cutaneous neurofibromas.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman presents with several painful subcutaneous nodules on her arms and legs. MRI of the spine and head shows no vestibular schwannomas. Biopsy of a nodule reveals a schwannoma. She has no café-au-lait spots or neurofibromas. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Multiple painful schwannomas without vestibular involvement is diagnostic of schwannomatosis. NF2 requires bilateral vestibular schwannomas or a combination with other tumours (especially meningiomas). NF1 features neurofibromas, not schwannomas. Multiple cutaneous neurofibromas are not schwannomas. Lipomatosis is not painful.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the paediatric clinic with six café-au-lait spots >5 mm in diameter. He has no other skin lesions, no freckling, and no family history of neurofibromatosis. Fundoscopy is normal. What is the single most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Perform a slit-lamp examination for Lisch nodules
Diagnose neurofibromatosis type 1 based on current features
Refer for genetic testing of the NF1 gene
questions.explanation: The child meets only one NIH diagnostic criterion (six café-au-lait spots). A slit-lamp examination to detect Lisch nodules could confirm a second criterion, aiding diagnosis without invasive testing. MRI and genetic testing are not first-line because the child may not meet diagnostic criteria yet.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of left-sided tinnitus and mild hearing loss. Pure tone audiometry shows asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the underlying diagnosis?
options: MRI of the internal auditory meatus with gadolinium
questions.explanation: MRI with gadolinium is the gold standard for detecting vestibular schwannoma, the hallmark of neurofibromatosis type 2. Audiometry and evoked potentials are suggestive but not diagnostic; CT is less sensitive for small tumours.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known neurofibromatosis type 1 develops severe back pain and progressive leg weakness over 3 weeks. He has a pre-existing plexiform neurofibroma in the same region. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the cause of his symptoms?
options: CT of the spine without contrast
MRI of the spine with contrast
questions.explanation: Acute neurological deterioration in a patient with a large plexiform neurofibroma raises concern for malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST). Contrast-enhanced MRI is the best non-invasive investigation to detect malignant transformation and plan biopsy. CT is less sensitive; PET-CT may be used later; biopsy is reserved for histological confirmation after imaging.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with bilateral sensorineural hearing loss. MRI shows bilateral vestibular schwannomas. What is the single most appropriate test to confirm a diagnosis of neurofibromatosis type 2?
options: MRI of the brain with contrast
questions.explanation: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are diagnostic of NF2, but genetic testing confirms the diagnosis, especially if atypical features are present or for genetic counselling. The other options are useful adjuncts but not confirmatory for the diagnosis itself.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old girl presents with learning difficulties and five café-au-lait spots >5 mm. She has no axillary or inguinal freckling, no Lisch nodules on examination, and no family history. What single investigation is most appropriate to support a diagnosis of neurofibromatosis type 1?
questions.explanation: Lisch nodules are a major diagnostic criterion for NF1 and are often present in childhood. A slit-lamp examination is non-invasive and can identify them, increasing the number of criteria met. Neuropsychological testing and MRI are not diagnostic; head circumference is not a criterion; skin biopsy is rarely needed.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child with neurofibromatosis type 1 presents with deteriorating vision. MRI shows a large optic pathway glioma compressing the optic chiasm. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: In symptomatic optic pathway gliomas, chemotherapy (vincristine/carboplatin) is first-line to avoid radiotherapy, which carries high risks of secondary malignancy and cognitive impairment in NF1 patients.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old child with neurofibromatosis type 1 has a large plexiform neurofibroma on the leg causing severe pain and functional impairment. Surgical resection is not possible due to involvement of major nerves. What is the most appropriate systemic therapy?
questions.explanation: Selumetinib, a MEK inhibitor, is NICE-approved for symptomatic, inoperable plexiform neurofibromas in children with NF1, shown to reduce tumour volume and improve symptoms.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with neurofibromatosis type 2 has a progressively growing left vestibular schwannoma and has recently developed mild hearing loss on that side. She wishes to preserve hearing. What is the most appropriate therapy?
questions.explanation: Bevacizumab, an anti-VEGF monoclonal antibody, has been shown to improve or stabilize hearing in some NF2 patients with progressive vestibular schwannomas and is considered first-line for hearing preservation.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with neurofibromatosis type 1 presents with a rapidly enlarging, painful mass in the thigh. MRI suggests a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST). What is the definitive first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For localized MPNST, wide local excision with negative margins is the primary curative treatment. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy are adjunctive or reserved for unresectable or metastatic disease.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with neurofibromatosis type 1 is found to have hypertension. Further investigations reveal a phaeochromocytoma. Which antihypertensive drug is contraindicated as first-line therapy before alpha-adrenoceptor blockade?
questions.explanation: Non-selective beta-blockers like propranolol are contraindicated in phaeochromocytoma until alpha-blockade is established, because unopposed alpha-receptor stimulation can precipitate a hypertensive crisis.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents to the paediatric clinic with multiple café-au-lait spots (six spots >5 mm), axillary freckling, and a freckled groin. No other features. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Request MRI brain to look for optic glioma
Start surveillance with annual blood pressure checks
Refer to paediatric ophthalmology for Lisch nodules
Perform genetic testing for NF1 mutation
Refer to clinical genetics for diagnostic assessment
questions.explanation: The child meets clinical diagnostic criteria for NF1 (≥6 café-au-lait spots plus one other feature). The most appropriate next step is referral to clinical genetics for formal diagnostic assessment and genetic counselling, rather than proceeding directly to genetic testing or imaging, which are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with neurofibromatosis type 1 develops persistent hypertension (BP 150/95 mmHg) on three readings during a routine clinic visit. He is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to nephrology for renal biopsy
questions.explanation: Hypertension in NF1 is often due to renal artery stenosis. The next step is to rule this out with renal artery duplex ultrasound before starting antihypertensives. Pheochromocytoma is less common but still possible; however, imaging for renal artery stenosis is the priority given the higher prevalence. Reassessment alone is inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with neurofibromatosis type 2 presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe headache, vomiting, and blurred vision. She has known bilateral vestibular schwannomas. On examination, she is drowsy with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 14, pulse 55 bpm, BP 160/95 mmHg, and bilateral papilloedema. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to neurosurgery for emergency shunt insertion
Arrange urgent CT head without contrast
Perform lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure
questions.explanation: Features suggest raised intracranial pressure (headache, vomiting, papilloedema, bradycardia, hypertension). The immediate next step is an urgent CT head to assess for hydrocephalus or mass effect before any other intervention. MRI takes longer, and lumbar puncture is contraindicated. Dexamethasone may be considered after imaging.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with neurofibromatosis type 1 presents with a rapidly enlarging, painful mass in his left thigh, palpable along the sciatic nerve. The mass has grown over 6 weeks and now measures 6 cm. There is no overlying skin change. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform urgent incisional biopsy of the mass
Arrange urgent MRI with contrast of the thigh
Refer to sarcoma MDT before any imaging
Start palliative radiotherapy for suspected malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour
questions.explanation: Rapidly enlarging, painful mass in NF1 raises suspicion of malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST). The next step is urgent MRI with contrast to evaluate for malignancy, which guides biopsy and MDT referral. Biopsy is usually performed after imaging, and immediate radiotherapy or reassessment without imaging is inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old pregnant woman (28 weeks gestation) with neurofibromatosis type 1 presents to the emergency department with a severe frontal headache, visual disturbances, and blood pressure 155/95 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 1+ protein. She is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform pre-eclampsia blood tests (FBC, U&E, LFTs) and send urine for protein:creatinine ratio
Arrange urgent MRI abdomen to exclude phaeochromocytoma
Give magnesium sulphate 4 g IV bolus
Refer to neurology for possible NF1-related cerebral vasculopathy
questions.explanation: Hypertension and headache in pregnancy with proteinuria must first be investigated for pre-eclampsia, a common and dangerous condition. Pre-eclampsia blood tests and urine protein assessment should be done before starting antihypertensives or imaging for rare causes. Magnesium sulphate is not given without a diagnosis of severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with neurofibromatosis type 1 presents to the emergency department with a rapidly enlarging, painful mass in the left thigh. She has several typical café-au-lait spots and numerous cutaneous neurofibromas. The mass has doubled in size over the past 2 months and is now very tender. Which single clinical feature most strongly suggests malignant transformation of a plexiform neurofibroma?
options: New onset of pain in the mass
Development of a new neurological deficit in the leg
Rapid increase in size of the mass
questions.explanation: Rapid increase in size is the most sensitive and specific red flag for malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST) in NF1. Pain alone can occur with benign lesions, but rapid growth is more concerning for malignant transformation.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known neurofibromatosis type 2 presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of acute low back pain and progressive weakness in both legs. On examination, he has a sensory level at T10, brisk knee jerks, and absent ankle jerks. He has not yet passed urine today. Which single examination finding is the strongest indicator of acute spinal cord compression requiring urgent neurosurgical referral?
questions.explanation: A clearly defined sensory level is the hallmark of spinal cord compression. Urinary retention and lower limb weakness are consistent but less specific, and back pain is common in benign conditions.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with neurofibromatosis type 1 attends the emergency department with episodes of severe headache, palpitations, and sweating. Her blood pressure is 200/120 mmHg during an episode and normal between episodes. She has café-au-lait spots and axillary freckling. Which life-threatening complication must be excluded urgently?
questions.explanation: Paroxysmal hypertension with the classic triad (headache, palpitations, sweating) in a patient with NF1 is highly suspicious for phaeochromocytoma. It can cause hypertensive crisis and requires urgent investigation and management.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy with newly diagnosed neurofibromatosis type 1 is brought to the emergency department by his mother because he has been vomiting for 2 days and has become lethargic. On examination, he has a right relative afferent pupillary defect and the 'setting sun' sign. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his presentation?
options: Optic pathway glioma causing obstructive hydrocephalus
questions.explanation: Children with NF1 are at risk of optic pathway gliomas. The combination of vomiting, lethargy, sunsetting eyes, and afferent pupillary defect suggests raised intracranial pressure due to a tumour obstructing CSF flow, most commonly an optic glioma in this setting.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a known history of neurofibromatosis type 1 presents with a sudden, severe 'thunderclap' headache that developed during a bowel movement. He has no focal neurological deficits but is photophobic. He is on warfarin for atrial fibrillation. Which of the following is the most likely life-threatening complication of NF1 in this scenario?
questions.explanation: NF1 is associated with intracranial aneurysms; thunderclap headache is classic for subarachnoid haemorrhage, especially with Valsalva. On anticoagulation, the risk is higher. Phaeochromocytoma can cause headache but typically not thunderclap; sinovenous thrombosis may present with headache but more gradual.
